title: DoorDash — Food Delivery — System Design
description: Design a food-delivery platform coordinating three parties — eater, restaurant, courier. The themes are the order lifecycle state machine, dispatching couriers to orders (geospatial matching), live tracking and ETA, and keeping the three-sided flow consistent.

nodes.label: Eater Client
nodes.description: Browses restaurants, places orders, tracks delivery live.

nodes.label: Courier Client
nodes.description: Receives dispatch offers, sends location, updates delivery state.

nodes.label: Restaurant Client
nodes.description: Receives and confirms orders, marks food ready.

nodes.label: Order Service
nodes.description: Owns the order lifecycle state machine and orchestration.

nodes.label: Order Store
nodes.description: Authoritative orders + state transitions, sharded by order id.

nodes.label: Dispatch / Matching Service
nodes.description: Assigns couriers to ready orders optimizing time/cost.

nodes.label: Courier Geo Index
nodes.description: Live courier locations indexed by geo cell for nearby lookup.

nodes.label: Tracking Service
nodes.description: Serves live order/courier location + ETA to the eater.

nodes.label: ETA / Routing Service
nodes.description: Estimates prep + travel time for matching and display.

nodes.label: Notification Service
nodes.description: Pushes state changes to all three parties.

nodes.label: Payment Service
nodes.description: Authorizes at order, captures on completion.

functional: Place an order from a restaurant; pay for it.
Send the order to the restaurant; track prep status.
Dispatch a courier to pick up and deliver the order.
Live-track the courier and show an ETA to the eater.
Drive all three parties through the order lifecycle with notifications.
nonFunctional: Order correctness: the lifecycle never gets into an inconsistent state across parties.
Real-time matching and tracking (location updates within seconds).
Reliable: a crash mid-order must not lose or duplicate orders/payments.
Scale to millions of orders/day and many concurrent live couriers.
Dispatch quality: minimize delivery time/cost while staying timely.

capacity.label: Match latency
capacity.note: Assign a courier promptly when food is ~ready.

api.description: Place an order; authorizes payment and starts the lifecycle.
Get live order status, courier location, and ETA.
Courier app reports its GPS position frequently.

dataModel.name: orders
dataModel.note: Authoritative order + lifecycle state, sharded by order id.

fields.name: state
fields.type: enum(placed, confirmed, preparing, ready, assigned, picked_up, in_transit, delivered, cancelled)

patterns.name: Order state machine
patterns.what: Model the order as explicit states with allowed transitions, persisted durably.
patterns.whenToUse: Multi-step, multi-party workflows that must stay consistent.

patterns.name: Geospatial courier index
patterns.what: Index live courier locations by geo cell for fast nearby lookup.
patterns.whenToUse: Matching that needs 'who's near here right now'.

patterns.name: Dispatch / matching
patterns.what: Score nearby available couriers (distance, ETA, load) and offer the order.
patterns.whenToUse: Assigning scarce mobile resources to time-sensitive tasks.

patterns.name: Location streaming + last-known
patterns.what: Ingest frequent GPS updates into a stream + a hot last-known store, not the DB per ping.
patterns.whenToUse: High-frequency location writes feeding tracking and matching.

patterns.name: Saga / compensation
patterns.what: Coordinate the multi-service flow with compensating actions on failure (refund, reassign).
patterns.whenToUse: Distributed workflows that can't be one ACID transaction.

patterns.name: Event-driven notifications
patterns.what: Emit state-change events that notify all three parties.

steps.title: 1. The Order Lifecycle State Machine
description: An order moves through explicit states: placed -> confirmed -> preparing -> ready -> assigned -> picked_up -> in_transit -> delivered (with cancel/refund branches). Model this as a state machine with allowed transitions, persisted durably as the source of truth.

Each transition is triggered by a party (restaurant confirms, courier picks up) and emits an event that notifies the others. Invalid transitions are rejected; the order store is authoritative for 'what state is this order in'.
This is the backbone — dispatch, tracking, and payment all hang off state transitions, and consistency means the state machine is the one place the truth lives.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you keep three parties (eater, restaurant, courier) agreeing on the order's status?

concepts.definition: Explicit states + allowed transitions persisted as the source of truth.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents inconsistent multi-party states and makes the flow auditable and recoverable.
concepts.example: ready -> assigned only after a courier accepts; transitions emit events.

whyNow: The state machine is the consistency backbone of a multi-party workflow; everything else triggers or reacts to its transitions.

recap.after: A durable, event-emitting order lifecycle all parties share.
recap.newRisk: Assigning a courier requires knowing who's nearby — a real-time geo problem.

steps.title: 2. Live Courier Locations (Geo Index)
description: Matching needs 'which available couriers are near this restaurant right now'. Couriers stream GPS frequently; index their live positions by geo cell (geohash/S2) so a nearby query is a cheap cell + neighbor lookup, not a scan.
Don't write every GPS ping to the order DB — that's a write storm. Update a hot geo index (and last-known store) on each ping; the index is derived/ephemeral, rebuilt from the stream.
This reuses the geospatial-indexing pattern (geohash cells, neighbor expansion) from proximity search, applied to moving couriers.
steps.decisionPrompt: 100K couriers each send GPS every 4 seconds. Where do those updates go, and how do you query 'nearby'?

concepts.definition: Live positions bucketed by geo cell for fast nearby lookup.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns 'find nearby couriers' into a cell range query at high update volume.
concepts.example: Courier ping -> update its geohash cell; dispatch reads the restaurant's cell + neighbors.

whyNow: Knowing who's nearby in real time is the precondition for dispatch; the geo index is the mechanism and a write-storm-avoidance decision.

recap.after: Live courier positions queryable by area, cheaply.
recap.newRisk: Knowing who's nearby isn't matching — you must pick the right courier and offer the order.

steps.title: 3. Dispatch: Matching Couriers to Orders
description: When an order is (nearly) ready, dispatch finds candidate couriers from the geo index and scores them: distance/ETA to the restaurant, current load, direction, fairness. It offers the order to the best candidate.

Offers are time-boxed: the courier accepts or it expires and dispatch re-offers to the next candidate — an offer/accept protocol, not a silent assignment. The assignment transitions the order to 'assigned'.
Timing matters: dispatch around when the food will be ready (using ETA), so the courier arrives just in time rather than waiting or being late — batching nearby orders is an optimization.
steps.decisionPrompt: Two orders become ready near the same courier at once. How do you assign without double-booking or starving one?

concepts.definition: Score nearby couriers and offer with a time-boxed accept, re-offering on decline/expiry.
concepts.whyItMatters: Respects courier agency and avoids assigning to an unavailable/unwilling courier.
concepts.example: Offer to nearest available; 15s to accept; else next candidate.

whyNow: Dispatch is the logistics heart; the offer/accept protocol and ready-time-aware matching are the senior signals distinct from pure ride-matching.

recap.after: Couriers matched to orders via scored, time-boxed offers.
recap.newRisk: The eater wants to watch the courier approach — live tracking + ETA.

traps.trap: Assigning a courier the instant the order is placed.
traps.why: The courier idles at the restaurant while food cooks, wasting capacity.
traps.instead: Dispatch timed to the food's ready ETA.

steps.title: 4. Live Tracking and ETA
description: Once assigned, the eater watches the courier move. The tracking service serves the courier's latest position (from the location stream's last-known store) plus a live ETA from the routing/ETA service.

Push updates to the eater (over a live connection) as the courier moves, throttled — they don't need every GPS point, just smooth movement and a stable ETA. The same location stream feeds both dispatch (matching) and tracking (display).
ETA blends remaining prep time, courier travel time, and traffic — recomputed as the courier progresses.
steps.decisionPrompt: Should the eater's app poll the courier's GPS directly, and should each ping hit the database?
whyNow: Live tracking is the headline eater experience; serving it from the stream/last-known store (not the DB) is the scaling insight.

concepts.definition: Serve the freshest cached position and push throttled updates, not raw GPS or DB reads.
concepts.whyItMatters: Smooth real-time tracking without DB write/read storms.
concepts.example: Courier ping -> last-known store -> throttled push to the eater's open connection.

recap.after: Live, ETA-annotated courier tracking for the eater.
recap.newRisk: The multi-service flow can fail mid-way (payment, restaurant cancel, courier drops) and must stay consistent.

steps.title: 5. Consistency Across Failures (Saga)
description: The flow spans order, payment, restaurant, and courier services — not one ACID transaction. Coordinate it as a saga: each step has a compensating action if a later step fails. Payment is authorized at order and captured on delivery; a cancel refunds the authorization.

Handle the real failure branches explicitly: restaurant rejects -> refund + notify; courier drops the order -> reassign; eater cancels before prep -> refund. Each transition is idempotent (keyed by order id + transition) so retries don't double-charge or double-assign.
The order state machine is the saga's ledger: it records where the flow is, so a crashed orchestrator resumes from the persisted state rather than losing or duplicating the order.
steps.decisionPrompt: Payment is authorized, the restaurant then rejects the order. What has to happen, and how do you avoid charging the eater?

concepts.definition: A multi-step distributed workflow where each step has a compensating undo on failure.
concepts.whyItMatters: Achieves cross-service consistency without a distributed ACID transaction.
concepts.example: Authorize -> restaurant rejects -> void authorization + notify eater.

whyNow: Multi-party consistency under partial failure is the correctness crux; the saga + idempotent transitions over the state machine is the standard answer.

recap.after: Failure branches compensated; idempotent, resumable transitions.
recap.newRisk: Meal-time spikes and location-write volume stress dispatch and the geo index.

failureDrills.scenario: The order orchestrator crashes after assigning a courier.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: On restart it reads the persisted order state and resumes; idempotency prevents a duplicate assignment.
failureDrills.mitigation: State machine as durable saga ledger + idempotent transitions.

failureDrills.scenario: A courier accepts then goes offline before pickup.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Dispatch detects the drop and re-offers the order to another courier.

steps.title: 6. Scaling Spikes, Geo Hotspots, and Regions
description: Demand spikes at meal times and concentrates geographically (a dense city at dinner). Shard orders by id and the geo index by region/cell so a hot city's load spreads; dispatch runs per-region so matching is local.

Absorb the location-write firehose with the stream + last-known store (not per-ping DB writes), and scale tracking/dispatch consumers off it. Batch nearby orders to one courier when it improves efficiency under load.
The geo index and last-known store are rebuildable from the location stream; orders are the durable truth. Degrade gracefully under extreme load (longer ETAs, surge) rather than failing matches.
steps.decisionPrompt: Friday 7pm in a dense city: order volume and courier pings both spike. What breaks first and how do you scale it?
whyNow: Closing on meal-time spikes + geo hotspots + regional sharding shows the design scales where it actually gets stressed.

recap.after: Regionally-sharded, spike-tolerant dispatch + tracking; rebuildable geo state.
recap.newRisk: Surge/ETA degradation tradeoffs and cross-region edge cases — bounded by per-region dispatch and graceful degradation.

bottlenecks.mitigation: Stream + last-known store; never per-ping DB writes.

bottlenecks.issue: Dispatch hotspot in a dense area at peak.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Per-region dispatch, order batching, surge to balance supply/demand.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Food Delivery Platform
finalDesign.description: An order moves through a durable state machine (placed->...->delivered) that emits events to notify eater/restaurant/courier; payment is authorized at order and captured on delivery, with saga compensation on failure branches. Couriers stream GPS into a location stream feeding a geo index (for dispatch) and a last-known store (for tracking). When food is ~ready, dispatch scores nearby couriers by ETA/load and offers the order time-boxed; the eater live-tracks the assigned courier with a recomputed ETA via push. Orders shard by id and the geo index by region with per-region dispatch; geo/last-known state is rebuildable from the stream while the order store stays authoritative.

functional.how: Location stream last-known + ETA service + push.

nonFunctional.how: Durable state machine + saga compensation + idempotency.
State machine as saga ledger; idempotent transitions.

say: Confirm three-sided: eater, restaurant, courier, coordinated by an order lifecycle.
Pin order consistency and real-time matching/tracking as the goals.
Note meal-time spikes and geo concentration.
Order state machine as the source of truth.
Geo index of live courier locations.
Live tracking + ETA from the location stream.
Tradeoffs: dispatch timing vs courier idle, location write volume, saga complexity.
Mention batching and surge pricing as extensions.

expectations: Models an order lifecycle and assigns couriers.
May miss geo indexing, saga compensation, and dispatch timing.
Designs the state machine + geo index + offer/accept dispatch.
Reasons about ready-time-aware dispatch and batching.
Discusses regional sharding, hotspots, and degradation precisely.

followUps: How would you batch multiple orders to one courier efficiently?
How do you set ETAs that blend prep time, travel, and traffic?
How would you add surge pricing to balance supply and demand?
How do you handle a courier who accepts then becomes unreachable?
How would you make dispatch fair across couriers over time?
